disease.name: Ulcerative Colitis
disease.note: Chronic inflammatory bowel disease confined to the colorectal mucosa. Characteristically starts in the rectum and extends proximally in a continuous, uniform fashion. Unlike Crohn's, it does not involve the small bowel (except backwash ileitis) and does not cause fistulas. In Kenya, UC is more common than Crohn's. Distinction from infectious colitis, especially amoebic colitis, is critical in the tropics. Long-standing disease carries significant colorectal cancer risk — surveillance colonoscopy is mandatory. Total proctocolectomy is curative.
pathophysiologyDetail: UC is primarily a Th2-mediated immune disorder driven by an aberrant CD4+ T-cell response to commensal gut microbiota in genetically susceptible individuals, with key roles for IL-5 and IL-13 in mucosal damage

Genome-wide association studies have identified >200 risk loci, including HLA-DR2/DRB1*0103, IL23R, IL10, ECM1, and NOD2 (protective in UC, contrary to Crohn's), highlighting the role of epithelial barrier function and immune regulation

Defective epithelial barrier integrity due to reduced tight junction protein expression (claudins, occludin) and altered mucin production (MUC2 reduction) allows increased bacterial antigen translocation across the colonic mucosa

Dysbiosis in UC is characterized by reduced microbial diversity, decreased Firmicutes (especially Faecalibacterium prausnitzii), and increased Proteobacteria and Enterobacteriaceae — similar to Crohn's but with distinct compositional features

Neutrophil infiltration into the crypt epithelium leads to crypt abscess formation, which is the histological hallmark of active UC — neutrophils accumulate in the crypt lumen, destroying crypt architecture

Goblet cell depletion (mucin depletion) results from the inflammatory cytokine milieu, reducing the protective mucus layer and exposing the epithelium to direct bacterial contact, perpetuating the inflammatory cycle

The inflammatory process in UC is confined to the mucosa and superficial submucosa — unlike Crohn's, the inflammation does not extend transmurally, which explains why fistulas and strictures are rare in UC
Continuous inflammation from the rectum proximally in a uniform, circumferential pattern reflects the specific homing of colonic-homing lymphocytes (α4β7 integrin) to gut-associated lymphoid tissue
The rectum is universally involved in UC — the disease always starts at the anal verge (dentate line) and extends proximally; rectal sparing strongly suggests Crohn's disease rather than UC

Pseudopolyps form when islands of regenerating mucosa surrounded by ulcerated epithelium create raised inflammatory projections — they indicate severe, longstanding inflammation and prior deep ulceration

The dysplasia-carcinoma sequence in UC follows the adenoma-like pathway, with p53 mutations occurring early and chromosomal instability leading to low-grade dysplasia → high-grade dysplasia → adenocarcinoma over 8-15 years

Primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) is associated with UC in 5-10% of patients — PSC-UC patients have a unique phenotype: often right-sided colitis with rectal sparing and a markedly increased colorectal cancer risk (4-fold higher than UC alone)

Anti-TNF therapy (Infliximab) works in UC primarily by inducing apoptosis of activated T-lymphocytes in the lamina propria, downregulating the Th2 cytokine cascade, and promoting regulatory T-cell function
The immune-epithelial axis in UC is amplified by IL-6 trans-signaling (soluble IL-6R binding to gp130 on epithelial cells), which promotes resistance to apoptosis and perpetuates chronic inflammation

Environmental triggers include recent smoking cessation (paradoxically protective during smoking), NSAID use, psychological stress, and dietary factors — these can precipitate first presentation or flares in susceptible individuals

In the Kenyan context, distinguishing UC from chronic amoebic colitis (Entamoeba histolytica infection) is critical, as amoebic colitis can persist for months and mimic UC endoscopically and histologically

landmarkTrials.intervention: Infliximab 5 mg/kg or 10 mg/kg vs placebo at weeks 0, 2, 6, then every 8 weeks
landmarkTrials.outcome: Clinical response at week 8: 61-69% infliximab vs 29-37% placebo (p<0.001). Clinical remission at week 54 (ACT 1): 33-36% infliximab vs 16% placebo (p<0.001). Lower colectomy rate in infliximab group at 1 year.
landmarkTrials.impact: Established anti-TNF therapy as effective in UC, not just Crohn's. Transformed the management of moderate-to-severe UC, reducing colectomy rates by 30-40%.

landmarkTrials.intervention: Infliximab + Azathioprine vs Infliximab alone vs Azathioprine alone
landmarkTrials.outcome: Steroid-free remission at week 16: 39.7% combination vs 22.1% infliximab alone vs 23.7% azathioprine alone (p=0.03). Mucosal healing also superior in combination group.
landmarkTrials.impact: Demonstrated the superiority of combination therapy in UC, mimicking the SONIC trial in Crohn's. Combination Infliximab + Azathioprine is now standard first-line biologic strategy.

landmarkTrials.outcome: Clinical response at week 6: 47.1% vedolizumab vs 25.5% placebo (p<0.001). Remission at week 52: 44.3% vs 15.7% (p<0.001). Mucosal healing: 51.6% vs 24.8%.
landmarkTrials.impact: First gut-selective biologic approved for UC. Particularly valuable in TB-endemic settings like Kenya because it does not cause systemic immunosuppression.

landmarkTrials.outcome: Clinical remission at week 52: 31.3% vedolizumab vs 22.5% adalimumab (p=0.006). Mucosal healing: 39.7% vs 27.7% (p<0.001). Vedolizumab superior on all endpoints.
landmarkTrials.impact: Landmark head-to-head trial showing superiority of Vedolizumab over Adalimumab for UC. Vedolizumab is now preferred as first-line biologic for moderate-severe UC, especially in TB-endemic regions.

landmarkTrials.outcome: Clinical remission at week 8: 15.5% ustekinumab vs 5.3% placebo (p<0.001). Maintenance at week 44: 43.8% ustekinumab (8-weekly) vs 24% placebo.
landmarkTrials.impact: Expanded therapeutic options for UC with another mechanism of action. Ustekinumab is effective in both anti-TNF naive and experienced patients.

landmarkTrials.population: 270 patients with acute severe UC failing IV steroids
landmarkTrials.outcome: No significant difference in colectomy-free survival at 1 year: 40% infliximab vs 38% cyclosporine (p=0.62). More adverse events with cyclosporine.
landmarkTrials.impact: Both agents are equally effective as rescue therapy. Infliximab is preferred due to better safety profile and easier administration.

landmarkTrials.population: 210 patients with UC undergoing surveillance colonoscopy
landmarkTrials.intervention: Chromoendoscopy (methylene blue spray) vs white-light endoscopy for dysplasia detection
landmarkTrials.outcome: Dysplasia detection rate: 30.4% chromoendoscopy vs 14.3% white-light (p=0.01). 4-fold more lesions detected with chromoendoscopy.
landmarkTrials.impact: Chromoendoscopy with targeted biopsies is now the standard for dysplasia surveillance in UC. Increases dysplasia detection and reduces need for random biopsies.

evidenceTable.indication: Gold standard — assess disease extent (Montreal classification), severity (Mayo score), and histology. Obtain biopsies from each segment (cecum, ascending, transverse, descending, sigmoid, rectum).
Non-invasive biomarker of intestinal inflammation. >250 mcg/g suggests active disease. Correlates with endoscopic activity. Useful for monitoring treatment response and predicting relapse.
Elevated CRP correlates with disease activity in extensive colitis but can be normal in proctitis (limited inflammatory mass). Serial CRP guides treatment response.
Positive in 60-70% of UC patients. Helps differentiate from Crohn's (ASCA positive). Atypical pANCA is specific for UC. Negative test does NOT exclude UC.
Mandatory before making UC diagnosis to exclude infectious colitis. In Kenya, amoebic colitis is the most important mimic. C. difficile testing is essential in hospitalized patients.
Assess colonic diameter in suspected toxic megacolon. Loss of haustrations (lead pipe sign) indicates chronic disease. Limited sensitivity for inflammatory activity.
Non-invasive assessment of disease extent and activity. Useful when colonoscopy is incomplete or contraindicated. Identifies mural thickening, hyperenhancement, and comb sign.
Screen for primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) — affects 5-10% of UC patients. Elevated ALP and GGT suggest PSC. MRCP confirms diagnosis.
Enhanced dysplasia detection during surveillance colonoscopy. Spray dye on colonic mucosa — dysplastic areas appear irregular. Targeted biopsies of abnormal areas.
Indicated if upper GI symptoms present or if differential from Crohn's is needed. UC does NOT involve the stomach or duodenum — any upper GI involvement suggests Crohn's.

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Clinical History — Chronic bloody diarrhea (>4 weeks), tenesmus, urgency, nocturnal symptoms, cramping abdominal pain. Prolonged course distinguishes IBD from infectious colitis (usually <2 weeks).

Step 2: Exclude Infection — Stool culture (×3), Ova & Parasites (×3), C. difficile toxin, Amoeba serology. In Kenya, this step is critical — amoebic colitis can exactly mimic UC endoscopically. Do NOT start steroids until infection is excluded.
Step 3: Blood Tests — CBC (anemia, thrombocytosis), CRP, albumin, LFTs (screen for PSC). Elevated CRP and thrombocytopenia suggest severe disease. Low albumin is a poor prognostic sign.
Step 4: Fecal Calprotectin — Elevation >250 mcg/g supports IBD over IBS. Useful for baseline and monitoring. Normal calprotectin makes active UC unlikely.

Step 5: Colonoscopy with Ileal Intubation — Gold standard for diagnosis. Inspect the entire colon and terminal ileum. Document: extent (proctitis, left-sided, extensive/pancolitis), severity (Mayo endoscopic score), and continuous inflammation from the rectum.

Step 6: Multiple Biopsies from Each Segment — Take 2-4 biopsies from each colonic segment (6 segments: cecum, ascending, transverse, descending, sigmoid, rectum). Histology: crypt abscesses, mucin depletion, basal plasmacytosis, architectural distortion.

Step 7: Classify Extent (Montreal Classification) — E1 (proctitis): limited to rectum. E2 (left-sided): to splenic flexure. E3 (extensive): beyond splenic flexure. Extent determines treatment and cancer surveillance interval.

Step 8: Assess Severity (Truelove & Witts) — Mild: <4 stools/day, no blood, normal vitals. Moderate: 4-6 stools/day, minimal systemic disturbance. Severe: >6 bloody stools/day + fever, tachycardia, anemia, or elevated CRP. Fulminant: >10 stools/day, systemic toxicity, colonic dilation.
Step 9: Initiate Treatment — Based on extent and severity. Stricturing and penetrating complications are rare in UC — if present, consider Crohn's disease instead.

Step 10: Dysplasia Surveillance — Start surveillance colonoscopy 8 years after diagnosis. Every 1-3 years depending on risk factors. Chromoendoscopy with targeted biopsies is the standard. Random biopsies every 10 cm with separate jars.

managementPearls.pearl: Before diagnosing UC in any patient in Kenya, three infections MUST be excluded: amoebic colitis (stool microscopy + serology), schistosomiasis (stool for ova), and tuberculosis (Quantiferon or biopsy). Steroids given to a patient with amoebic colitis can trigger fulminant amoebic megacolon with 50% mortality.

Acute severe UC (Truelove & Witts severe) is a medical emergency requiring hospital admission. IV Hydrocortisone 100mg QID is first-line. Response is assessed on day 3 using the Oxford criteria: stool frequency >8/day OR stool frequency 3-8/day with CRP >45 mg/L predicts 85% need for colectomy.

Rescue therapy for steroid-refractory acute severe UC: Infliximab 5mg/kg single dose or Cyclosporine 2mg/kg/day IV. The CONSTRUCT trial showed no difference in outcomes between the two. Infliximab is preferred due to easier transition to maintenance therapy.

Mucosal healing (Mayo endoscopic score 0-1) is the therapeutic target in UC — it predicts sustained clinical remission, lower hospitalization rates, reduced colectomy risk, and lower colorectal cancer risk. Fecal calprotectin <100 mcg/g is a useful surrogate marker.

Cancer surveillance in UC: Start colonoscopy 8 years after diagnosis, then every 1-3 years depending on risk. High-risk patients (PSC, family history of CRC, extensive colitis, active histologic inflammation) require annual chromoendoscopy. Random biopsies alone miss 50% of dysplastic lesions.

Restorative proctocolectomy with IPAA (J-pouch) is the elective surgical procedure of choice for UC. It removes all diseased mucosa, cures the disease, and avoids a permanent stoma. Do NOT offer this procedure in Crohn's disease — the risk of pouch failure is unacceptably high.

Pouchitis occurs in 30-50% of patients after IPAA. Most cases respond to Metronidazole 400mg TDS or Ciprofloxacin 500mg BD for 2 weeks. Chronic pouchitis may require probiotics (VSL#3), oral budesonide, or biologic therapy. Pouchoscopy with biopsy is essential to confirm diagnosis and exclude Crohn's of the pouch.
managementPearls.evidence: Shen et al., Inflamm Bowel Dis 2018

managementPearls.pearl: Primary sclerosing cholangitis in UC patients dramatically increases colorectal cancer risk (4-fold) and requires annual surveillance starting from the time of PSC diagnosis, regardless of UC duration. These patients also have higher rates of pouchitis after IPAA.

Vedolizumab is an excellent first-line biologic choice in Kenya because of its gut-selective mechanism. Unlike anti-TNF agents, Vedolizumab does not increase the risk of TB reactivation — a major advantage in a TB-endemic country.

In patients presenting with UC for the first time after age 50, always consider ischemic colitis and NSAID-induced colitis in the differential. Ischemic colitis typically presents with acute onset of pain out of proportion, while NSAIDs can cause a diffuse colitis that resolves on drug withdrawal.

Corticosteroid dependency (inability to taper below 10mg/day prednisolone within 3 months without relapse) is an indication to escalate to biologic therapy or consider colectomy. Chronic steroid use causes osteoporosis, diabetes, cataracts, and increases infection risk — avoid prolonged use.

operativeNuances.nuance: In emergency colectomy for acute severe UC, perform total abdominal colectomy with end ileostomy — save the rectum. Do NOT attempt primary anastomosis. The pelvic dissection in the acute setting carries high risk of ureteric injury and presacral bleeding. The rectal stump is managed as a Hartmann pouch and can be treated postoperatively with Mesalamine enemas.
operativeNuances.evidence: Fazio et al., Dis Colon Rectum 1995

operativeNuances.nuance: In restorative proctocolectomy with IPAA, the J-pouch should be 15 cm in length (two 15 cm limbs of terminal ileum). Longer pouches (>20 cm) have poor emptying; shorter pouches (<10 cm) have high stool frequency. The pouch is constructed using a linear stapler inserted through the apex of the J.
operativeNuances.evidence: Utsunomiya et al., Dis Colon Rectum 1980

operativeNuances.nuance: During proctectomy for UC, dissect in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane close to the rectal wall (mesorectal plane for cancer; close rectal dissection for benign UC). Preserve the pelvic autonomic nerves (hypogastric nerves, sacral splanchnic nerves, pelvic plexus) to maintain sexual and urinary function.
operativeNuances.evidence: Heald et al., Lancet 1986; Lindsey et al., Dis Colon Rectum 2004

operativeNuances.nuance: The stapled ileoanal anastomosis (2 cm above dentate line) has better functional outcomes than hand-sewn anastomosis with mucosectomy (which excises the anal transition zone). The stapled technique preserves anal sensation, reducing soiling and urgency. However, in UC with rectal dysplasia, mucosectomy is required to remove all at-risk mucosa.

A diverting loop ileostomy should be created at the time of IPAA to protect the anastomosis during the healing phase. Ileostomy closure is performed 8-12 weeks later after contrast enema confirms anastomotic integrity. Loop ileostomy reduces symptomatic anastomotic leak from 10-15% to 3-5%.
operativeNuances.evidence: Remzi et al., Dis Colon Rectum 2006

operativeNuances.nuance: In IPAA surgery, the mesentery of the terminal ileum often needs to be mobilized to the duodenum (Kocherization) to gain enough length for the pouch to reach the anal canal without tension. Tension on the pouch-anal anastomosis is the leading cause of anastomotic leak and pouch failure.

prognosis.naturalHistory: UC follows a relapsing-remitting course with 70-80% of patients experiencing a flare within 2 years of diagnosis. At 10 years: 50% have clinical remission on medication, 30% have persistent activity, 20% have undergone colectomy. The disease remains confined to the rectum in 30%, extends to left-sided in 40%, and to pancolitis in 30%. Extension occurs in 10-30% over 10 years.
prognosis.recurrenceRates: Flare rate: 30-50% per year without maintenance therapy. With optimal 5-ASA maintenance: 20-30% per year. On biologics (Anti-TNF, Vedolizumab): 10-20% per year. Colectomy rate: 10-15% at 10 years, 20-25% at 20 years. After IPAA: cumulative pouchitis incidence 30% at 1 year, 50% at 10 years. Pouch failure (need for permanent ileostomy): 5-10% at 10 years.
prognosis.cancerRisk: Colorectal cancer risk in UC: 2-fold increase at 10 years, 8-fold at 20 years, 18-fold at 30 years compared to the general population. Absolute risk: 2% at 10 years, 8% at 20 years, 18% at 30 years. Risk is highest with: pan colitis (5-15× risk of proctitis alone), duration >8 years, PSC (4-fold additional risk), family history of CRC, active histologic inflammation, and post-inflammatory polyps.
prognosis.qualityOfLife: UC significantly impairs quality of life during flares — urgency, incontinence, and frequency affect work, social life, and sleep. In remission, most patients have normal quality of life. After IPAA, quality of life is significantly improved compared to before surgery, with 90% of patients satisfied. Stool frequency after IPAA averages 4-8 per day with 1-2 at night. Minor incontinence (soiling) affects 10-20% but major incontinence is rare.
prognosis.mortality: Overall mortality in UC is similar to the general population — the disease is not associated with increased mortality in well-managed patients. Mortality is increased in: acute severe colitis requiring emergency colectomy (3-5% mortality), toxic megacolon (10-20%), colorectal cancer (standard CRC mortality), and complications of immunosuppression (infections, PSC). The risk of death from colectomy for elective indications is <1%.
prognosis.diseaseModifyingFactors: Factors associated with worse prognosis: young age at onset (<20 years), extensive colitis (E3), severe endoscopic activity (Mayo 3), high CRP and low albumin at presentation, frequent flares, smoking cessation, PSC, family history of CRC, and poor adherence to maintenance therapy. Protective factors: adherence to maintenance 5-ASA therapy (reduces CRC risk by 40-50%), successful IPAA (cures disease), chromoendoscopy surveillance (detects dysplasia early), and biologic therapy achieving mucosal healing.

presentingComplaints: Bloody diarrhea with mucus — frequent small-volume stools with blood
Persistent and urgent need to pass stool (tenesmus and urgency)
Cramping lower abdominal pain, often left-sided
Unintentional weight loss and chronic fatigue
Waking up at night with the urge to pass stool

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Toxic megacolon/perforation/massive hemorrhage/fulminant -> SEVERE; mild-moderate colitis -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: How long have you had bloody diarrhea — days, weeks, months, or years?
How many times do you pass stool in a day? How many of those contain blood?
Do you have a strong sudden urge to run to the toilet (urgency)?
Do you ever feel you need to pass stool but nothing comes out (tenesmus)?
Does the diarrhea wake you up at night?
Have you lost weight unintentionally in the past 6 months?
Do you have cramping abdominal pain before passing stool?
Do you have any family members with ulcerative colitis or Crohn's disease?
Have you ever been diagnosed with inflammatory bowel disease before?
Do you smoke? Did you recently quit smoking?
Have you been on any medications for colitis (Mesalamine, steroids, biologics)?
Have you taken antibiotics recently or been hospitalized?
Do you have any joint pain or swelling?
Do you have any skin rashes, especially tender red nodules on your shins?
Have you noticed any eye redness or inflammation?
Have you ever had a colonoscopy with biopsies?
Have you taken any traditional remedies or herbs for this condition?
Do you have any other chronic illnesses?
Are you taking any medications like blood thinners or NSAIDs (Ibuprofen)?
Has anyone in your family had colon cancer?
Have you ever been diagnosed with HIV or any chronic infection?
What is your typical diet? Do you eat traditional foods like ugali, githeri, or more processed foods?
Have you noticed yellowing of the eyes or itching (possible PSC)?
Do you have any problems with your bile ducts or liver?

examination_findings.finding: Left lower quadrant tenderness on deep palpation
Guarding if severe inflammation or toxic megacolon developing
Pallor suggesting chronic iron deficiency anemia
Signs of toxicity: fever, tachycardia, hypotension
Abdominal distension if toxic megacolon developing
Perianal examination — typically normal (no fistulas, unlike Crohn's)
Digital rectal exam — tender, blood on glove, may feel granular mucosa
Temporal wasting, muscle wasting from chronic disease
Erythema nodosum (tender red nodules on shins)
Joint swelling or tenderness (sacropelvic joints more common)
Toxic megacolon triad: distension, tenderness, decreased bowel sounds
Perianal excoriation from frequent diarrhea — skin breakdown around anus
Oral aphthous ulcers — indicating active mucosal inflammation
Signs of chronic liver disease — icterus, spider nevi, hepatomegaly (suggesting PSC)
Joint effusion or sacroiliac tenderness — extraintestinal manifestation

bedside_scores.name: Truelove & Witts Severity Index
bedside_scores.interpretation: 0-2: mild; 3-4: moderate; 5-6: severe. Severe has 30% risk of colectomy in the same admission.

riskFactors.note: Smoking is paradoxically protective — quitting increases risk

subtypes.name: Ulcerative Proctitis
subtypes.management: Topical 5-ASA (Mesalamine suppositories 1g daily or enemas 4g). Topical corticosteroids if insufficient response (Hydrocortisone enema). Oral 5-ASA if refractory. Prognosis better than extensive colitis.

subtypes.name: Left-Sided Colitis
subtypes.management: Oral + topical 5-ASA. Corticosteroids if moderate-severe (Prednisolone 40mg/day). Immunomodulators (Azathioprine 2-2.5mg/kg) for steroid-dependent. Biologics (Anti-TNF Infliximab or Adalimumab) for refractory disease. Colectomy if medical therapy fails.

subtypes.name: Pancolitis / Extensive Colitis
subtypes.management: IV corticosteroids (Hydrocortisone 100mg QID). IV fluids, electrolyte correction. DVT prophylaxis. Rescue therapy: IV Cyclosporine 2mg/kg/day or Infliximab 5mg/kg. Total abdominal colectomy with end ileostomy if medical therapy fails (25-40% rate). Restorative proctocolectomy (IPAA) after recovery. Surveillance colonoscopy for colorectal cancer starts 8 years after diagnosis.

subtypes.name: Ulcerative Colitis with PSC
subtypes.management: Same medical therapy as UC. Increased surveillance: annual colonoscopy with chromoendoscopy from time of PSC diagnosis. Higher colectomy rate for dysplasia. After IPAA: higher pouchitis rate. UDCA (Ursodeoxycholic acid) for PSC — may reduce CRC risk. Liver transplant evaluation if PSC progresses.

bedside.name: Digital rectal examination
bedside.indication: assess sphincter tone, blood on glove, rule out mass

bedside.name: Stool culture, Ova/Parasites, C. difficile toxin
bedside.indication: mandatory to exclude infectious causes before diagnosing UC

laboratory.name: CRP
laboratory.indication: inflammatory marker — correlates with disease activity

laboratory.name: Albumin
laboratory.indication: severity marker — low albumin indicates severe disease

laboratory.name: Fecal Calprotectin
laboratory.indication: intestinal inflammation — correlates with endoscopic activity

laboratory.name: pANCA/ASCA serology
laboratory.indication: pANCA+ in 70% of UC; helps differentiate from Crohn's

laboratory.name: Liver function tests
laboratory.indication: screen for PSC (primary sclerosing cholangitis — associated with UC)

imaging.name: Colonoscopy with multiple biopsies
imaging.indication: gold standard — assess extent, severity, continuous inflammation, dysplasia screening

imaging.name: Abdominal X-Ray (supine)
imaging.indication: assess for toxic megacolon, thumbprinting, colonic diameter

imaging.name: CT Abdomen with contrast
imaging.indication: assess severity, rule out perforation, assess for toxic megacolon

imaging.name: MR Colonography
imaging.indication: research/experimental — non-invasive assessment of colitis activity

interpretation_rules.mayo_endoscopic_score: 0: normal/inactive; 1: mild (erythema, decreased vascularity); 2: moderate (friability, erosions); 3: severe (spontaneous bleeding, ulcers). Used for treatment decisions.
interpretation_rules.fecal_calprotectin_correlation: >250 mcg/g correlates with active endoscopic disease. Useful for monitoring treatment response. Target <100 mcg/g for mucosal healing.
interpretation_rules.dysplasia_surveillance: Start colonoscopy surveillance 8 years after diagnosis. Every 1-3 years depending on risk factors (PSC, family history, extent). Chromoendoscopy improves dysplasia detection.
interpretation_rules.acute_severe_colitis_criteria: >6 bloody stools/day + CRP >30 mg/L = severe. If no improvement in 3-5 days of IV steroids, need rescue therapy (Infliximab/Cyclosporine) or colectomy.

management.initial: Confirm diagnosis (colonoscopy + histology). Exclude infection. Assess disease extent and severity. Start medical therapy based on extent and severity. Nutritional assessment. Smoking cessation advice (patients who smoke should not start — but quitting may trigger flare).
management.definitive: Mild-moderate: Oral 5-ASA (Mesalamine 2-4.8g/day) + topical 5-ASA for proctitis/left-sided. Step up to corticosteroids (Prednisolone 40mg/day tapering) if no response. Moderate-severe: IV corticosteroids. Rescue therapy (Infliximab 5mg/kg or Cyclosporine 2mg/kg/day) if no response in 3-5 days. Maintenance: Azathioprine 2-2.5mg/kg/day or 6-MP. Biologics (Infliximab, Adalimumab, Vedolizumab, Ustekinumab) for maintenance. Surgical: Total abdominal colectomy with end ileostomy for acute severe colitis. Restorative proctocolectomy with IPAA for elective setting.
management.postop: After colectomy: stoma care education. Nutrition optimization. Plan for completion proctectomy and IPAA after 3-6 months. After IPAA: Pouch function monitoring (stool frequency 4-8/day normal). Pouchitis (inflammation of pouch) occurs in 30-50% — treat with antibiotics (Metronidazole or Ciprofloxacin). Long-term surveillance: annual pouchoscopy for dysplasia.

pathways.criteria: <6 stools/day, no systemic toxicity, CRP <30, limited extent
pathways.treatment: Topical 5-ASA (suppositories/enemas). Oral 5-ASA for extensive. Topical steroids if no response. Step to oral steroids if needed.

pathways.criteria: >6 bloody stools/day, systemic symptoms, CRP >30, anemia, extensive disease
pathways.treatment: Hospitalization for IV steroids (Hydrocortisone 100mg QID). IV fluids, DVT prophylaxis. If no response in 3-5 days: rescue therapy (Infliximab 5mg/kg or Cyclosporine). Colectomy if rescue fails.

pathways.criteria: colonic dilation >6cm, systemic toxicity, severe bleeding
pathways.treatment: NBM, NG tube, IV fluids, IV steroids. Rescue therapy. Urgent colectomy if no improvement in 24-48h. Total abdominal colectomy with end ileostomy.

operative_templates.procedure: Total Abdominal Colectomy with End Ileostomy (Emergency)
operative_templates.indication: Acute severe or fulminant UC with failure of medical therapy
operative_templates.approach: Open midline laparotomy or laparoscopic if stable
steps: Midline laparotomy, careful entry
Mobilize right, transverse, left colon and sigmoid
Protect ureters throughout
Divide ileum 5cm from ileocecal valve
Divide colonic mesentery (ileocolic, right, middle, left colic, sigmoid vessels)
Divide rectosigmoid at sacral promontory
Oversew/staple rectal stump, leave in pelvis (attached to presacral fascia)
Deliver terminal ileum as end ileostomy (right iliac fossa)
Copious lavage
Close midline wound

operative_templates.indication: Elective surgery for UC after recovery from acute episode, for dysplasia, or medically refractory disease
operative_templates.incision: Laparoscopic ports + Pfannenstiel for extraction and pouch creation
steps: Diagnostic laparoscopy, assess for synchronous pathology
Complete mobilization of colon as per colectomy
Complete protectomy in the TME plane (preserve pelvic nerves)
Divide rectum at the top of the anal canal
Create ileal J-pouch (two 15cm limbs of ileum, side-to-side stapled)
Bring pouch down to the anal canal
Create stapled ileoanal anastomosis (or hand-sewn with mucosectomy)
Test anastomosis with air
Create diverting loop ileostomy (protective)
Place pelvic drain
Close wounds

patient_education.explanation: Ulcerative colitis is a chronic disease where the immune system attacks the lining of the large bowel, causing inflammation, ulcers, and bleeding. It always starts in the rectum and can extend upwards to involve the entire colon. Unlike Crohn's disease, it only affects the inner lining (mucosa) and does not cause tunnels (fistulas). The disease cannot be cured by medication alone, but surgery to remove the colon and rectum is curative. In Kenya, we must always test for amoeba and other infections first, as they can look exactly like UC.
patient_education.warningSigns: Return to hospital if: you have more than 6 bloody stools per day with fever, your abdomen becomes swollen and painful, you feel dizzy or faint, you vomit and cannot keep fluids down, or you have a sudden increase in ileostomy output causing dehydration.
patient_education.activity: You can lead a normal active life with UC. During flares, rest more. Between flares, regular exercise is encouraged. If you have an ileostomy, most activities are possible including farming and boda-boda riding — use a support belt. Pelvic floor exercises are helpful if you have urgency after IPAA.
patient_education.diet: During flares: low-fiber, bland diet to reduce irritation — rice, bananas, white bread, cooked vegetables, fish, chicken. Between flares: balanced diet with all food groups. Some people find dairy, spicy foods, or raw vegetables trigger symptoms. Keep a food diary. Drink 8 glasses of water. Avoid alcohol and smoking. If you have an ileostomy, chew food well and avoid foods that block the stoma (nuts, popcorn, maize seeds).
patient_education.medication: IBD medications are for long-term control — take them even when you feel well. Stopping medications causes flares. If you are on steroids, follow the tapering schedule exactly — do not stop suddenly. Biologics (Infliximab, Adalimumab) require regular injections/infusions — do not miss doses. All patients need regular blood monitoring. Vaccinations (flu, pneumonia, hepatitis B) are important, but avoid live vaccines if on immunosuppression.
patient_education.followUp: You will need lifelong follow-up. Colonoscopy surveillance starts 8 years after diagnosis and is done every 1-3 years. After IPAA surgery, annual pouchoscopy. Blood tests every 3-6 months. You should see both a gastroenterologist and a colorectal surgeon regularly.

followup.before_discharge: Ensure medical therapy plan (steroid taper, biologic schedule). Stoma care teaching. Nutritional assessment. DVT prophylaxis plan. Family education.

after_discharge.1_week: Wound check. Stoma function assessment. Review histology if available. Ensure outpatient medications arranged.
after_discharge.2_weeks: Clinical assessment — symptoms improving. Adjust medications. Coordinate with gastroenterology. Plan elective IPAA timing (3-6 months post-colectomy).
after_discharge.6_weeks: Full recovery evaluation. Discuss IPAA surgical plan. Nutritional optimization. Smoking cessation. Steroid taper completion check.
after_discharge.3_months: Pre-IPAA optimization. Pouch function counseling. Arrange surgical date. Discuss risk of pouchitis.
after_discharge.6_months: Undergo IPAA with diverting ileostomy if planned. Otherwise continued surveillance with annual colonoscopy and cancer surveillance.

followup.when_to_return: Immediately if: severe diarrhea with blood >6/day, fever, abdominal pain, distension, vomiting, high ileostomy output causing dizziness, or any acute worsening of symptoms.
